result:
In the spirit of writing freely, here is a long, wandering meditation—part memoir, part essay, part notebook—on the things that keep nudging me awake at night: the problem of remembering, the joy of tiny things, and the stubborn conviction that somewhere inside the daily mess there is a hidden latch that, if pressed, swings open a small, immaculate door.

I. The smell of graphite at 2:00 a.m.

I keep a handful of Ticonderoga pencils in a chipped jam jar beside the bed. When the house is finally still—no hum of the dishwasher, no phantom creaks of hot-water pipes—I twist on the little clip-on reading lamp, sharpen one pencil with the brass wedge I bought on a school trip to Quebec in 1998, and watch the cedar curls fall like dark commas onto the quilt. The graphite smells faintly of rain on warm asphalt, a scent that tugs me back to childhood summers when my parents let me stay up late to finish a drawing of a stegosaurus. I believed, with the feverish certainty only seven-year-olds possess, that if I got every plate along its spine exactly right, the dinosaur would breathe itself alive on the page and lumber off in search of a Jurassic salad bar.

Nothing ever walked off the paper, yet here I am, decades later, still coaxing things to life with graphite. Sometimes I write words; sometimes I shade cubes; sometimes I simply hold the pencil upright like an antenna and let the graphite talk to the dark. The doctors call it “a rich interior life,” my partner calls it “your adorable bat-hours,” and I, who have never successfully kept a houseplant alive, call it compost: I am piling scraps in the belief that if I keep turning them, something unexpected will sprout.

II. A brief taxonomy of forgetting

When I was twenty-four I lived above a bakery in a German city whose name I sometimes forget. The bakery opened at four, and the scent of rye would rise through the floorboards like a Lutheran hymn. I was an au pair, tasked with teaching two brothers, Florian and Felix, how to conjugate Shakespeare while their parents conjugated corporate mergers. Every Saturday they handed me an envelope of cash and every Tuesday they asked what I had done with their sons’ accents.

What nobody asked was what I was forgetting in order to be there. I forgot the names of my high-school teachers. I forgot the taste of a certain blackberry pie my mother made only once, the summer the creek flooded and the power stayed out for three days. I forgot, catastrophically, the chords to the song my grandfather used to sing when he shaved on the back porch, a tune I promised myself I would archive forever.

Memory is a zero-sum carnival. Each new country you enter demands a souvenir from the last. The hippocampus, overstuffed, quietly jettisons cargo: your first phone number, the smell of your grandmother’s umbrella. The process is painless—you don’t even notice the splash—but years later you find yourself staring at an umbrella in an antique shop, gripping the handle, weeping over a loss you can’t articulate.

III. The museum of lost odors

I have, therefore, begun a private museum. Admission is free; hours are irregular. The collections are invisible. In it I curate the following extinct scents:

1. The cool aluminum smell of the seats in a 1987 Pontiac Grand Prix, heated to vinyl sigh by Alabama sun.  
2. The peppery breath of our classroom iguana after he ate the teacher’s geranium.  
3. The yeasty hush inside a sleeping bag during a thunderstorm at Camp Kickapoo, 1991.  
4. The whiff of gun oil on my great-uncle’s field jacket, stored in a cedar closet beside a box of Purple Heart citations.  
5. The ghost of Jergens lotion (original cherry-almond) on the hands of a stranger who once held my face after I fainted on a subway platform in Boston.

Sometimes I uncap a jar of linseed oil, close my eyes, and travel instantaneously to the art room at Easley Elementary where Mrs. Whetstone let us print monotypes with our palms. Other times I press my nose to the warm screen of an old television and inhale nothing—static has no odor—but the posture itself summons the Saturday-morning smell of carpet shampoo and buttered oatmeal. The brain is a reckless time machine; it needs only a breadcrumb to fling you backward.

IV. On the pleasure of tiny, perfect tools

I nurse an unreasonable affection for objects that are precisely the size they need to be. Take the KUM Masterpiece pencil sharpener, a rectangle of magnesium no larger than a matchbox. Inside it hides a curved blade of the sort once forged in Solingen for daggers. One twist and the wood peels away in a single, unbroken helix, as if the pencil secretly wished to be a unicorn. I own four, because I live in dread of the day the company is bought by conglomerates that will swap magnesium for cheese-grade plastic.

Or the platinum-plated Kaweco Sport fountain pen, barely longer than a lipstick. Posted, the cap snaps onto the barrel and the pen becomes a full-grown writing instrument; uncapped, it shrinks back into pocket-able anonymity, like Clark Kent. I fill mine with De Atramentis “Alexander Hamilton” ink, a blue-black so saturated it looks almost conspiratorial.

Why the obsession with tiny perfect tools? They are the sworn enemies of imprecision, which is the parent of regret. A dull blade chews the pencil; a cheap pen burps ink onto the birthday card. The correction tape that follows is a white scar, a reminder that you were careless with a moment. Tiny perfect tools promise a stay against that—an assurance that whatever you are making will not leak, wilt, snap, or sour before it reaches the person you are secretly hoping to astonish.

V. Micro-joys, or how to survive Monday

Here is an incomplete syllabus of microscopic happinesses I have collected, each tested under rigorous Monday conditions:

1. Peeling an orange in one continuous spiral, then flicking the coil so it springs like a Slinky.  
2. Opening a book to the exact page you intended, without resort to the index.  
3. Stepping off the moving walkway at the airport in sync with the beep, so your shoe lands precisely on the yellow grid.  
4. Discovering that the barista accidentally made your drink a size larger, and you have exactly the right amount of loyalty points left to cover it.  
5. Hearing a stranger hum the same song that has been looping in your skull all day, a phenomenon I call “radio déjà vu.”  
6. Finding a forgotten ten-dollar bill in the pocket of last winter’s coat, and realizing it’s the exact amount needed to buy the overpriced tulips you were coveting at the corner bodega.  

A micro-joy is not the same as gratitude. Gratitude is a steak dinner eaten slowly with full mindfulness of the cow. A micro-joy is a single Skittle that turns out to be the flavor you hoped: instantaneous, weightless, gone before guilt arrives. String enough of them together and you have a necklace invisible to the outside world but glinting softly against your collarbone all day.

VI. The ethics of eavesdropping

I am an unrepentant eavesdropper. On trains, in cafes, in the echoing stairwell of my dentist’s office, I collect fragments of conversation the way children pocket sea glass. A recent haul:

—She said, “If you’re going to break up with me, do it before the farmers’ market, because I don’t want to cry in front of the honey guy.”  
—He said, “The algorithm thinks I’m pregnant because I bought pickles and batteries in the same cart.”  
—A child whispered to a stuffed giraffe: “Don’t worry, I have a plan for when the floor turns to lava.”  

Is it theft? Possibly. But words uttered in public are like pigeons: they belong to whoever bothers to look up. Besides, eavesdropping trains you in the art of narrative collision—how to slam two unrelated desires together and watch sparks. The honey guy becomes a minor character in a breakup; the algorithm becomes a Greek chorus foretelling pregnancy; the lava floor becomes a metaphor for adult life, for climate anxiety, for the moment the mortgage rate adjusts.

VII. The paradox of archiving

Because I fear forgetting, I archive compulsively: ticket stubs, wine labels, the foil inside a chocolate bar embossed with the face of a 19th-century poet. I photograph graffiti before the city paints it over; I scan the gas-station receipts that still smelled of coffee when I shove them into shoeboxes. The result is a private Smithsonian in the basement, a low-grade mold-scented shrine to the moment I once stood in line for a pretzel.

Yet the more I archive, the less I inhabit. The camera, as Susan Sontag warned, is a sublimated weapon. Each click is a tiny evacuation: I was here, but not in the here-ness; I was already curating the memory, cropping out the blister on my heel. Paradoxically, the only artifacts I revisit are the ones I failed to capture: the northern sky above the bakery in that German town, a green so delicate it looked like the idea of color rather than color itself. I have no photo, no pencil sketch, no postcard. The sky exists only as a bruise on the soft tissue of recollection, and therefore I carry it everywhere, like contraband diamonds sewn into the hem of a coat I never take off.

VIII. The law of reciprocity with inanimate objects

I believe objects keep score. Not in a sentient Toy-Story way, but quid-pro-quid at the molecular level. If you treat a pair of boots kindly—polish, cedar trees, rest days—they will carry you across a decade of puddles without a blowout. Neglect them and they disintegrate exactly one week before the first blizzard, forcing you to dash through slush in canvas sneakers, cursing entropy.

This belief extends to books. If you read a book too fast, flipping pages like a dealer in Vegas, the plot will slip your mind within a fortnight. But if you pause to copy a sentence into a commonplace book, if you let the words linger on your tongue like hard candy, the story will return to you years later, unbidden, as you wait in line at the DMV. The book, grateful for your attention, lends you its syntax at the exact moment you need to say something tender to a heart that is breaking in your kitchen.

IX. A short treatise on handwriting

I was taught the D’Nealian method, a transitional script whose letterforms lunge toward cursive but never fully arrive, like teenagers at a dance pretending not to care. My third-grade teacher, Mrs. Braswell, wielded a rubber stamp that read “Neatness counts.” She marched up and down the aisles leaving crimson smacks on worksheets whose loops dared to wobble. To this day, whenever I write the word “giraffe,” the tail of the final f curls in self-defense, anticipating the stamp.

Handwriting is the ghost of the hand. Forensic scientists can tell if you were angry, exhausted, lying. A love letter penned on a train ride smells subtly of diesel even through the envelope; a resignation letter drafted at midnight carries the microscopic tremor of second thoughts. I write all first drafts by hand because typing feels like firing words into a void; handwriting is burial and resurrection—you push the ink into the paper, and months later the paper yields it back, the way soil offers up the ghost of last year’s tulips.

X. The loneliness of the long-distance reader

Reading is the only intimacy that leaves no DNA. You can spend six hundred pages inside another mind, know precisely how the narrator eats grapefruit, how she flinches at the word “moist,” how she conceals grief beneath a joke about dryer lint—and yet she will never know you. Turn the last page and you are expelled, amniotic, into silence. The book spine clicks shut like a taxicab door; the character rolls on without you, unaltered, while you wander the kitchen in search of a snack that will taste like reunion.

Some readers chase the high with book clubs, those weekly AA meetings for the literarily heartsore. Others, bolder, become writers themselves, reverse-engineering their own blood-brain barrier, hoping a stranger will someday close their book and stare at the wall with the hollow echo of evicted kin. I oscillate between the two: I want to be known without the mortifying experience of being seen. Hence the pencil jar, the notebooks, the midnight sharpening—an attempt to build a bridge out of cedar and graphite and cross it before anyone notices I have left.

XI. The cartography of childhood dens

Every child is a cartographer of secret spaces. Mine was beneath the dining-room table, draped with an old wool overcoat of my father’s that smelled of pipe tobacco and weather. Inside, the world resolved to a rectangle of lamp-light filtered through tweed. I kept three objects: a flashlight shaped like a penguin, a spiral notebook, and a petrified shark tooth my cousin claimed was from a megalodon but was probably from a reef shark who died of boredom. I wrote newspapers for an imaginary republic whose population fluctuated depending on how many socks were lost in the laundry. The lead story one week: “President Penguin Declares War on Dryer.”

Adults lose access to such dens. We acquire square footage but surrender enclosure; we buy sofas but cannot crawl under them without considering spinal alignment. Yet the impulse persists. In airplane bathrooms I study how the door lock slides into its chrome mouth; in hotel corridors I count ceiling tiles and imagine crawling into the space above. The desire is not claustrophilia but sovereignty: a place where you control the borders, where the border patrol is your own small fist.

XII. On the necessity of impractical languages

I am learning Latin not because I expect to negotiate with the Vatican but because I crave linguistic pockets that have no native speakers to correct my accent. When I decline “rosa, rosae,” I feel like a guest walking through a house where the owners died centuries ago, leaving behind elegant frescoes and half-eaten bread. The silence is total; no one will burst in yelling that I have mispronounced the dative plural. The language is done evolving, a frozen waterfall in which you can see, suspended, the fish of ancient jokes, the pebbles of lost arguments.

Dead languages are time machines you pedal yourself. Translate a line of Ovid and you momentarily rent a room overlooking the Tiber where laundry flaps between brick apartments; a drunk poet is singing about a girl who broke his lyre. Meanwhile, your downstairs neighbor bangs on the ceiling because you have been chanting pentameter at 3:00 a.m. again. The walls between centuries are thinner than drywall.

XIII. Ghosts I have installed

Everyone installs ghosts. You walk into a room, flick on the light, and for a fraction of a second you see your ex standing there in the posture of apology, even though he lives in Portland now with a woman who teaches aerial yoga. The brain is a projector that accepts any reel: the father who swore he’d show up to the recital; the friend who died in a car crash the week you fought over nothing; the version of yourself who stayed in the German bakery town, became a translator of 17th-century hymns, and drinks elderflower syrup on balconies.

I try to keep my ghosts in good repair. I sit them down, pour imaginary tea, ask what they came to remind me of. Sometimes they only want to say: You are still angry. Sometimes they confess: We are bored, invent something new. Once, the ghost of my never-born daughter requested a bedtime story. I told her about the planet where pencils grow on trees; she fell asleep mid-sentence, a curl of cedar in her translucent fist.

XIV. The philosophy of small, good actions

The world is ending in at least four separate ways, but the kettle still clicks off at 212°F, and tea leaves still unscroll into dragon shapes if you pour the water slowly. I do not know how to stop the arctic from hemorrhaging ice, but I can return the grocery cart to the corral even when nobody is watching, thereby sparing the teenager who retrieves them a small misery. I can tip in cash because you cannot garnish what you cannot trace. I can leave the blackout curtains cracked so the delivery driver can see the house number without unbelting his seatbelt.

These actions are laughably small, but they accumulate like cedar shavings under the sharpener. Somewhere a tired woman finishes her shift fifteen seconds earlier because I corralled the cart; she gets home in time to see her kid take the first wobbly bicycle ride around the block. The kid grows up and designs an improved battery cell. The battery powers a fleet of electric trucks. The trucks deliver pencils to schoolchildren who will sketch dinosaurs that will not walk off the page but will still matter in ways the trucks cannot fathom. All this because I pushed a cart and left a light on. Causation is a drunk butterfly; you never know which flap will level or gentle the storm.

XV. How to end without ending

There is no ending. The graphite keeps spinning. The ghosts keep sipping tea. Somewhere a dinosaur is patiently waiting for a child to finish the plates along its spine. Somewhere a loaf of rye is rising, and the scent is traveling through the floorboards of a life I no longer live, into the nostrils of a woman I no longer am, but who is still, even now, pushing a cart through the dawn-lit aisles of a supermarket that stocks seventeen brands of elderflower syrup.

I sharpen the pencil again. The curl falls, a comma that refuses to close the sentence. The dark is still dark, but darker now with graphite gleaming like the faintest constellation. Outside, the first garbage truck of the morning huffs and sighs, the municipal breath of a city waking up. I click off the lamp. The jar stands guard. The words—those that have survived the long night—settle into paper like passengers buckling up for an unscheduled stop.

Tomorrow I will forget some, remember others, misplace the sky, find a metro ticket from 2003, and press it into the shoebox of fragments. The day after, I will open the box, inhale the ghosts, and start again.

XIV. The philosophy of small, good actions

The world is ending in at least four separate ways, but the kettle still clicks off at 212°F, and tea leaves still unscroll into dragon shapes if you pour the water slowly. I do not know how to stop the arctic from hemorrhaging ice, but I can return the grocery cart to the corral even when nobody is watching, thereby sparing the teenager who retrieves them a small misery. I can tip in cash because you cannot garnish what you cannot trace. I can leave the blackout curtains cracked so the delivery driver can see the house number without unbelting his seatbelt.

These actions are laughably small, but they accumulate like cedar shavings under the sharpener. Somewhere a tired woman finishes her shift fifteen seconds earlier because I corralled the cart; she gets home in time to see her kid take the first wobbly bicycle ride around the block. The kid grows up and designs an improved battery cell. The battery powers a fleet of electric trucks. The trucks deliver pencils to schoolchildren who will sketch dinosaurs that will not walk off the page but will still matter in ways the trucks cannot fathom. All this because I pushed a cart and left a light on. Causation is a drunk butterfly; you never know which flap will level or gentle the storm.

XV. How to end without ending

There is no ending. The graphite keeps spinning. The ghosts keep sipping tea. Somewhere a dinosaur is patiently waiting for a child to finish the plates along its spine. Somewhere a loaf of rye is rising, and the scent is traveling through the floorboards of a life I no longer live, into the nostrils of a woman I no longer am, but who is still, even now, pushing a cart through the dawn-lit aisles of a supermarket that stocks seventeen brands of elderflower syrup.

I sharpen the pencil again. The curl falls, a comma that refuses to close the sentence. The dark is still dark, but darker now with graphite gleaming like the faintest constellation. Outside, the first garbage truck of the morning huffs and sighs, the municipal breath of a city waking up. I click off the lamp. The jar stands guard. The words—those that have survived the long night—settle into paper like passengers buckling up for an unscheduled stop.

Tomorrow I will forget some, remember others, misplace the sky, find a metro ticket from 2003, and press it into the shoebox of fragments. The day after, I will open the box, inhale the ghosts, and start again.
